ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Nasi Padang; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002700) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Nasi Padang code; A00KP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed rice plate with protein and condiments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Nasi Padang or equivalent Minangkabau rice dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of Nasi Padang chicken set is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed white rice and 150 g spiced chicken with sambal and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Padang (Chicken Set) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Nasi Padang (Chicken Set), CR ID F05100094. Saturated fat estimated from HPB composite data for coconut-milk chicken curry dishes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and numerous micronutrients are not individually reported by HPB for this composite dish and are set to -1 as documented in the notes field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Asian-style) GI ~72
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Padang chicken set as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white rice (Asian-style), GI 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). The chicken, coconut milk, and sambal components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the composite meal; however, the white rice GI of 72 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (72 × 13.06 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 32.9 g available carbohydrate per serving; GL = (72 × 26.1) / 100 ≈ 18.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 330 mg/100 g; estimated 1155 mg per standard 350 g serving, approaching 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation of the chicken curry and use sambal sparingly, aiming to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg by pairing with low-sodium vegetable sides.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base carries a GI of 72 (high); a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 26 g available carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~18.8, which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–120 g, increase the chicken and vegetable proportion, and consider requesting brown rice substitution to lower the glycemic impact of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 3.5 g/100 g from coconut milk in the curry base; a 350 g serving contributes approximately 12.3 g saturated fat, which is 55–60% of the recommended daily limit of 20 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced coconut milk or a lighter curry base, limit the serving to 250 g, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetable sides to mitigate saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk contributes saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g); sambal contains nightshade-family chilli which may aggravate inflammation in nightshade-sensitive RA patients; fibre is low at 1.73 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with nightshade sensitivity should avoid or minimise sambal; request reduced coconut milk in the curry preparation and add extra vegetable sides to improve anti-inflammatory fibre and micronutrient intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Padang (Chicken Set) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish with Minangkabau (West Sumatran) origins, widely served across Singapore's Malay and Padang food stalls. The dish consists of steamed white rice served alongside spiced chicken — typically prepared as ayam rendang (dry coconut-milk curry) or ayam kari (wet curry) — accompanied by sambal chilli and optional vegetable sides. The chicken is slow-cooked with a fragrant spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, kaffir lime leaves, shallots, and garlic, simmered in coconut milk until the sauce is rich and aromatic. The rice is soft and neutral, providing a starchy base that absorbs the bold, savoury, mildly spicy flavours of the chicken and sambal. Per 100 g, the dish provides 181 kcal, 13.64 g protein, 8.6 g fat, and 13.06 g carbohydrate, making it a moderately energy-dense, high-protein meal. Sodium is elevated at 330 mg/100 g, and saturated fat from coconut milk is approximately 3.5 g/100 g. The white rice base carries a high glycemic index (~72), making portion control important for blood glucose management. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI white rice base, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from coconut milk. It is a culturally significant dish representing Minangkabau culinary heritage and is a staple of Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (CR ID F05100094) at confidence 0.95 with review_status 'reviewed' and provenance 'canonical'. Saturated fat estimated from HPB composite data for coconut-milk chicken curry dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white rice (Asian-style) as the dominant carbohydrate component, which is a well-established and defensible analogous estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, individual micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Nasi Padang chicken set are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database (F05100094) for this specific composite dish, and no equivalent composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. The HPB Foodo database reports only the seven core nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) for this record; all remaining fields therefore carry the -1 sentinel value indicating data not available from the consulted approved sources. Potassium value of 210 mg/100 g is carried forward from the input record's health_suitability clinical reasoning, consistent with HPB composite estimates for similar dishes. Saturated fat of 3.5 g/100 g is estimated from HPB composite data for coconut-milk chicken curry dishes and is not directly reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific record. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Padang chicken set as a composite dish. Input record glycemic_index field was set to 0 with confidence 0.42 and source 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with the approved University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. All core macros (protein_g 13.64, fat_g 8.6, carbohydrate_g 13.06) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
